destination.name: Tokaj
destination.hero_image_credit: Photo by Balint Miko on Unsplash
destination.hero_image_alt: Tokaj vineyard landscape on volcanic hillsides in northeast Hungary
destination.tagline: Aszú gold from volcanic hillsides, 1737 royal decree, 2002 UNESCO inscription
destination.overview:
The Tokaj wine region (Tokaj-Hegyalja) occupies 5,500 hectares of volcanic hillsides in northeast Hungary at the confluence of the Bodrog and Tisza rivers. Formally classified by royal decree of Charles VI in 1737, it is one of the world's oldest legally defined wine appellations, predating Bordeaux classifications by more than a century. In 2002 UNESCO inscribed the Tokaj Wine Region Historic Cultural Landscape on its World Heritage List, recognising the integration of winemaking, landscape and built heritage across 27 villages.

The region's signature wine, Tokaji Aszú, is made from grapes individually selected after they have been shrivelled and concentrated by Botrytis cinerea (noble rot), encouraged by the autumn mists that rise from the two rivers. Botrytised berries, called aszú, are macerated in fermenting base wine or must and the resulting sweet wine graded by sweetness: since 2013, the minimum legal level for the Aszú label is 5 puttonyos (approximately 120 g/L residual sugar). The ultra-rare Tokaji Eszencia, free-run juice from unpressed aszú berries, reaches 500 to 900 g/L residual sugar and takes years to ferment.

Furmint dominates plantings at approximately 70%, providing the backbone for both Aszú and the dry white revival that gathered pace after 1989. Hárslevelű (linden leaf), Sárga Muskotály (Muscat Blanc à Petits Grains), Zéta, Kabar and the near-extinct Kövérszőlő complete the permitted varieties. The post-communist revival, led by pioneering estates such as Szepsy from 1989 onwards and joined by foreign investors at Royal Tokaji, Disznókő and Oremus in the early 1990s, transformed the region from state-controlled bulk production into a quality-focused appellation with strong international recognition.

The village of Mád has emerged as the modern winemaking hub, concentrating the highest density of quality producers around the historic dűlő (single-vineyard) sites of Nyulászó, Szt. Tamás, Birsalmás and Betsek. Tarcal, to the south, hosts the first-class classified Mézes Mály (Honey Meadow). Tolcsva shelters Oremus and the deep tunnel cellar network dating to the 13th century. Thirty years after the private revival began, Tokaj is Hungary's most internationally exported wine region by volume and the country's most recognised wine appellation abroad.

faqs.question: What is Tokaji Aszú and how is it made?
faqs.answer: Tokaji Aszú is a sweet white wine made from individually hand-selected botrytised grapes (aszú berries) from Furmint, Hárslevelű and Sárga Muskotály vines. Noble rot (Botrytis cinerea) concentrates sugar, glycerol and acidity in the berries. The aszú berries are macerated in fermenting must or base wine, then pressed and fermented slowly. Since 2013 the minimum sweetness level for the Aszú label is 5 puttonyos, corresponding to approximately 120 g/L residual sugar.

faqs.question: What makes Tokaj a UNESCO World Heritage Site?
faqs.answer: UNESCO inscribed the Tokaj Wine Region Historic Cultural Landscape in 2002, recognising the exceptional integration of winemaking tradition, historic village architecture, cellar networks and the natural landscape across the 27 villages of the appellation. The region also holds the distinction of being one of the world's oldest formally classified wine appellations, dating to the royal decree of Charles VI in 1737.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Tokaji Aszú and Tokaji Eszencia?
faqs.answer: Tokaji Eszencia is the ultra-sweet free-run juice that seeps naturally from aszú berries under their own weight without pressing, reaching 500 to 900 g/L residual sugar. It ferments very slowly over years due to its extreme sugar content and produces a wine of exceptional concentration and longevity. Tokaji Aszú, by contrast, is made by pressing the aszú berries into a base wine or must and reaches a minimum of 120 g/L residual sugar in the 5 puttonyos tier.

faqs.question: Which village is the best base for visiting Tokaj wineries?
faqs.answer: Mád is the preferred base for visiting the highest concentration of quality private producers, including Szepsy, Royal Tokaji, Holdvölgy, Barta Pince and Demeter Zoltán, all within walking distance of each other along the village's volcanic hillsides. Tokaj town itself is the gateway for visitors arriving by train and offers the Tokaj Wine Region Visitor Centre plus several historic cellars on Bem Street.

faqs.question: What food pairs well with Tokaji Aszú?
faqs.answer: Tokaji Aszú 5 and 6 puttonyos are classic partners for Hungarian foie gras (libamáj), blue cheese, Roquefort and aged hard cheeses. The wine's high acidity balances the richness of these pairings. Lighter sweet Aszú and dry Szamorodni pair well with fruit tarts, crème brûlée and fresh fruit desserts. Dry Furmint accompanies freshwater fish from the Tisza, pike-perch (fogas), halászlé fish soup and mild aged sheep's cheese.

faqs.question: When is the best time to visit Tokaj?
faqs.answer: Late September through October is harvest season and the most atmospheric time to visit; botrytis berry selection runs through October and into November in the best years. Spring (April to May) offers the cellar visits without crowds and the opportunity to taste the previous vintage shortly after bottling. The Tokaj Spring Wine Festival (Tokaji Borfesztivál) typically takes place in May.
